subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can count coins to tell the total amount of money, up to $10.00.
Your child can match coins of the same type, like pennies, nickels, dimes, or quarters, when looking at a group of coins.
Your child can say how much a nickel, dime, or quarter is worth in another way.
Your child can tell which group of coins is worth more or less.
Your child can match groups of coins to pictures that show their decimal value.
Your child can write the value of pennies, nickels, or dimes using dollar or cent signs.
Your child explores place values using objects like base-10 blocks and coins.
Your child explores place values using objects like pennies and dimes.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Money — Counting, Comparing, and Place Value
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on counting and comparing coins and their values, but two standards emphasize using coins to understand place value, introducing a slight variation in the concept.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can sort flat shapes by things they share, like the number of sides or angles.
Your child can sort flat shapes and say what they have in common, like sides, corners, or color.
Your child can tell the difference between round and square shapes, or between a ball and a box shape.
Your child can put flat shapes into categories by looking at their features.
Your child can name flat shapes like circles, squares, and stars.
Your child understands that if a shape belongs to a big group, it also has the properties of smaller groups inside it.
Your child can recognize and name a sphere, cube, cone, and cylinder.
Your child understands which activities happen in the morning and which happen in the afternoon.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Geometry — Classifying and Naming Shapes
coherence_reason: Most standards are about classifying and naming shapes, both 2D and 3D, but one standard is about distinguishing times of day, which is unrelated.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can use objects or pictures to show repeated addition for simple multiplication problems up to 3.
Your child can use blocks or models to compare numbers up to 99.
Your child can look at two groups of objects (up to 10) and tell which group has more.
Your child can tell what hour it is using a digital or regular clock.
Your child can look at numbers and pictures of amounts (1-10) and say which is bigger.
Your child can put numbers like 10, 100, 1,000, and 10,000 in order from smallest to largest.
Your child can put numbers up to 10 in order from smallest to largest.
Your child can use blocks or models to compare two numbers up to 99 and see which is bigger.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Numbers and Operations — Comparing and Ordering Numbers
coherence_reason: Standards are about comparing, ordering, and modeling numbers, but also include time-telling and repeated addition for multiplication, which are distinct topics.
split_topics: Comparing and ordering numbers (using objects, models, symbols)

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands how to use two number lines that cross at zero to find points using pairs of numbers called coordinates.
Your child can graph points on a coordinate plane to solve problems.
Your child can plot points on a graph in the first quadrant and explain what the points mean in real situations.
Your child understands how Alabama’s five geographic regions affected where settlers moved in the early 1800s.
Your child can put points on a graph to help solve real-world and math problems.
Your child can plot points on the first quadrant of a coordinate plane and explain what the points mean in real situations.
Your child can draw and identify points, lines, line segments, rays, different types of angles, and lines that are perpendicular, parallel, or intersecting.
Your child can draw shapes on a coordinate plane using points and find side lengths when points share an x or y coordinate.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Coordinate Plane — Graphing Points and Solving Problems
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on graphing and interpreting points on the coordinate plane, but some statements address unrelated topics like Alabama’s geography and basic geometry concepts, indicating two distinct clusters.
split_topics: Graphing and Interpreting Points on the Coordinate Plane
Basic Geometry Concepts (Points, Lines, Angles, and Shapes)

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can create two number patterns from rules, find relationships between them, and graph pairs of matching numbers.
Your child can find and continue number patterns.
Your child can make, describe, and continue simple number patterns that use easy addition or subtraction rules.
Your child can continue a simple AB pattern using pictures, objects, or familiar symbols.
Your child can continue a simple pattern like ABAB, ABC, or ABBA using shapes, numbers, or objects.
Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.
Your child can look at patterns and relationships between numbers.
Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Patterns — Identifying, Continuing, and Analyzing Number Patterns
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on creating, identifying, and continuing numerical or visual patterns, but some emphasize simple visual patterns (e.g., AB, ABC) which are more primary grade content, while others focus on analyzing number relationships and problem-solving patterns typical of grade 5.
split_topics: Visual or object patterns (AB, ABC, ABBA sequences)

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can measure volume by counting unit cubes using different units like cubic centimeters or cubic inches.
Your child can find the volume of solid shapes made by joining two rectangular boxes by adding their volumes.
Your child can find the volume of a rectangular box by filling it with unit cubes and by multiplying its length, width, and height.
Your child understands volume relates to multiplication and addition and can solve problems about volume.
Your child can find the volume of a box by counting how many unit cubes fit inside.
Your child recognizes volume as a property of solid shapes and understands how to measure it.
Your child understands a cube with sides one unit long is called a unit cube and has a volume of one cubic unit.
Your child can figure out how much space a box holds by counting small cubes that fit inside.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Measurement — Understanding and Finding Volume of Rectangular Prisms
coherence_reason: All standards focus on understanding, measuring, and calculating the volume of rectangular prisms using unit cubes and formulas.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can measure how long or heavy things are using standard units.
Your child can choose the right tool to measure how long or heavy something is.
Your child can use the right tool to measure how long or heavy something is using inches, feet, ounces, or pounds.
Your child can convert between different units of measurement within the same system.
Your child can change measurements from one unit to another within the same system and use these changes to solve multi-step problems.
Your child can measure, estimate, and convert between different units of length, weight, volume, and temperature in both English and Metric systems to solve real problems.
Your child can convert between units that measure the same thing within one measurement system.
Your child can use a conversion chart to solve problems that change measurements like miles to yards or hours to minutes, using whole numbers.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Measurement — Units, Tools, and Conversions
coherence_reason: All standards focus on measurement skills, specifically using and converting standard units, but there is minor variation in emphasis on estimating and solving multi-step problems.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements with fractions and use fraction math to solve problems from the plot.
Your child can find which group in a bar graph or picture graph has the most or least when the differences are very clear.
Your child can read and understand information from charts, tables, graphs, tallies, and pictographs.
Your child can figure out what kind or how much information is needed to answer a question, solve a problem, or fill in a chart or pictograph.
Represent and interpret data.
Your child can show and understand data using a picture, line plot, or bar graph.
Your child can look at different views about how World War II and what happened after affected the economy, politics, and society.
Your child can make daily schedules, calendars, and timelines to organize events.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Data — Representation and Interpretation
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on representing and interpreting data in various graph forms, but a few standards refer to historical analysis and organizing events in timelines, which are distinct sub-concepts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can divide a group into equal parts to solve a problem.
Your child can split a group of objects into two equal groups.
Your child can show division by sharing things out fairly and equally.
Your child can tell the difference between a whole and one-half.
Your child can put together two groups that have the same number of objects in each.
Your child can tell if a shape or object is split into 1 or 3 equal parts.
Your child can divide a rectangle into rows and columns of equal squares and count how many squares there are.
Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts and use words like halves, fourths, and quarters to describe them, understanding that more parts mean smaller pieces.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Fractions — Equal Parts and Division of Shapes
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on dividing groups or shapes into equal parts and understanding concepts of halves, fourths, and equal shares, but a couple also reference distinguishing wholes from halves or combining equal groups, which adds minor variation.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.
Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.
Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.
Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.
Your child can choose and use the right tools to help solve math problems.
Collaboration
Your child understands what rights people have and what they should do as citizens in your community and state.
Your child understands how to think carefully and solve problems.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Mathematical Practices — Problem Solving and Reasoning
coherence_reason: Most standards are about mathematical thinking and problem-solving, but one standard is about citizenship which is a distinct topic.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Model with mathematics.
Your child can write simple math expressions to show calculations and understand what the expressions mean without solving them.
Your child can use parentheses, brackets, or braces in math expressions and solve problems that include these symbols.
Your child can write math expressions with numbers and explain what they mean.
Your child understands and uses the chain rule to find derivatives of combined functions.
Your child finds derivatives of functions defined with parameters and uses implicit differentiation in various problems.
Your child can use parentheses in math expressions and correctly solve expressions that include them.
Your child can write and understand math expressions.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Expressions — Writing and Interpreting Numerical Expressions
coherence_reason: Most standards deal with writing and interpreting numerical expressions, but two address calculus concepts like derivatives, which are not grade 5 topics.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can find the area of rectangles with fractional side lengths by counting small squares and by multiplying the side lengths.
Your child can find the area of triangles, special four-sided shapes, and other polygons by breaking them into simpler shapes.
Your child can find the area of rectangles with fractional sides by counting small squares and by multiplying the side lengths.
Your child can find the perimeter and area of rectangles with decimal side lengths using pictures and a calculator.
Your child can find the perimeter and area of rectangles with sides that are fractions or decimals using pictures and formulas.
Your child can find the area of rectangles with fractional sides by covering them with smaller rectangles and by multiplying the side lengths.
Your child can use rectangles and number lines to explore fractions.
Your child can work with fraction models that show area (like rectangles) and length (like number lines).
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Geometry — Area and Perimeter with Fractions and Decimals
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on finding area and perimeter of rectangles and polygons with fractional and decimal side lengths, but a few also mention use of number lines and general exploration of fractions which is a slight expansion of the main concept.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can point out 1/3 and 1/10 when shown pictures or models.
Your child can tell the difference between halves and fourths in a shape or model.
Your child can find and name halves and fourths in pictures or models.
Your child can find and name thirds and tenths in pictures or models.
Your child can recognize and name thirds and tenths when looking at a model.
Your child can find one-half and one-fourth in a picture or model.
Your child can tell time to the half or quarter hour using a digital or regular clock.
Your child can tell the difference between something that is empty and something that is full.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Fractions — Identifying Unit Fractions in Models
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on identifying unit fractions in models, but two address telling time and distinguishing empty/full, which are distinct concepts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can round decimals to any place value using their understanding of place value.
Your child can round numbers from 0 to 20 to the nearest ten using a number line.
Your child can round two-digit numbers (from 0 to 90) to the nearest ten using a number line.
Your child can round two-digit numbers to the nearest ten.
Attend to precision.
Your child can round decimals to any place value using their understanding of place values.
Your child can use rounding, estimating, and place value to understand multiplying and dividing numbers with decimals to the hundredths place.
Your child can round decimal numbers up to 9.99 to the nearest whole number or tenth, like rounding 1.7 to 2 or 2.36 to 2.4.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Place Value — Rounding Whole Numbers and Decimals
coherence_reason: The standards cover both rounding whole numbers (including two-digit numbers) and rounding decimals, with some references to estimation in multiplication/division, indicating two related but distinct subtopics.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands multiplication as resizing or scaling.

Your child can explain why multiplying by a fraction bigger than 1 makes a number bigger, and multiplying by a fraction less than 1 makes it smaller, and understand multiplying fractions by 1 keeps them the same.
Your child can compare the size of a multiplication result to one factor by looking at the other factor without actually multiplying.
Your child understands why Europeans explored and settled in Alabama and how this affected trade, health, and land.
Your child understands why multiplying by a fraction bigger than 1 makes a number bigger, and multiplying by a fraction smaller than 1 makes it smaller.
Your child can compare the size of a multiplication answer to one of the numbers being multiplied, without actually multiplying.
Your child understands multiplying a fraction by a number as taking parts of that number, using pictures to show this.
Your child understands multiplication as resizing or scaling numbers.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Multiplication — Scaling and Comparing Products
coherence_reason: All standards focus on interpreting multiplication as scaling, comparing product sizes based on factors, especially fractions, with only one unrelated standard about European settlement.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands a fraction as dividing the top number by the bottom number and can solve word problems using this idea.
Your child uses equivalent fractions to help add and subtract fractions.
Your child understands multiplying a fraction by a whole number as taking parts of that whole number or by multiplying and dividing in steps.
Your child can do addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with multi-digit whole numbers and decimals to hundredths.
Your child can understand and calculate what happens when you divide a whole number by a unit fraction.
Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths using models and strategies, and explain how they solved the problems.
Your child can solve real problems multiplying fractions and mixed numbers using pictures or equations.
Your child can add and subtract fractions with different denominators by changing them to have the same denominator first.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Fractions and Decimals — Operations and Problem Solving
coherence_reason: The cluster includes both operations with fractions (including conceptual understanding and word problems) and operations with decimals, which are related but distinct subtopics.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can say if a number from 0 to 10 is more, less, or equal to another number.
Your child can compare two decimals to thousandths and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.
Your child can compare numbers up to 100 using the symbols =, <, and >.
Your child can compare two objects to see which one is longer or heavier when the difference is obvious.
Your child can compare numbers up to 100 using the symbols <, >, and =.
Your child can compare two decimals up to the thousandths place and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.
Your child can put decimal numbers up to 9.99 on a number line, put them in order, and compare which are bigger or smaller.
Your child can compare two decimals up to thousandths and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Number Sense — Comparing and Ordering Decimals
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on comparing decimals to the thousandths, but several refer to comparing whole numbers and some to comparing measurable attributes (length/weight), so there are at least two distinct sub-concepts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can read, write, and compare decimals up to thousandths.
Your child understands how the place value system works.
Your child can read and write decimals to thousandths in numbers, words, and expanded form.
Your child understands how the War of 1812 and the Creek War affected Alabama socially, politically, and economically.
Your child understands how place value works in numbers.
Your child can read, write, and compare decimals up to the thousandths place.
Your child can read and write decimals to the thousandths place in numbers, words, and expanded form.
Your child can place, order, and compare numbers with decimals up to the thousandths place.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Decimals — Reading, Writing, and Comparing Decimals to Thousandths
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on decimal reading, writing, and comparison to thousandths using place value, but one standard is unrelated (about the War of 1812 and Creek War in Alabama).

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands how multiplying or dividing by powers of 10 changes the number and decimal point, and can explain the patterns.
Your child understands patterns when multiplying or dividing by powers of 10 and can explain how the decimal point moves.
Your child can practice multiplying and dividing single-digit numbers by 0.1 and 0.01.
Your child can multiply and divide numbers with decimals by 0.1 and 0.01 accurately.
Your child can use whole-number exponents to show powers of 10 and explain patterns when multiplying or dividing by powers of 10.
Your child can explain how multiplying or dividing by powers of 10 changes the number and where the decimal point moves.
Your child can explain how multiplying or dividing by 10, 100, or 1000 changes a number and where the decimal point moves.
Your child can write a math sentence to show a situation involving multiples of 10.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Place Value — Multiplying and Dividing by Powers of 10
coherence_reason: All standards focus on understanding, explaining, and applying multiplication and division by powers of 10, including patterns with decimal placement and use of exponents.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that the number of zeros in numbers like 10, 100, or 1,000 helps show which number is bigger or smaller.
Your child can tell if a single-digit number is closer to 0 or 10 on a number line.
Your child knows that each digit in a number is ten times bigger than the digit to its right and one tenth the size of the digit to its left.
Your child knows that in a number, each digit is ten times bigger than the digit to its right and one-tenth the size of the digit to its left.
Your child understands how the value of a digit changes when it moves one place to the left in decimal numbers up to 9.99.
Your child understands how the value of a digit changes when it moves left or right in a number with decimals.
Your child understands that in a multi-digit number, each digit is 10 times bigger than the digit to its right and one-tenth the size of the digit to its left.
Your child understands that each digit in a number is one-tenth the value of the digit to its left.
canonical_name: Grade 5 Math: Place Value — Powers of Ten and Digit Value
coherence_reason: All standards focus on place value and the relationship of digits in base-ten numbers, but one standard about proximity to 0 or 10 on the number line is only loosely related.
